long_thesis.english_markdown:
# Daily FI Market Intelligence: 2026-06-24 NY Close

## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The June 24 NY close gave fixed-income investors a cleaner duration signal than the previous session, with strict conditions around the breadth of the move. The defining feature was the distribution of the Treasury move. The 2-year yield fell 5 bp to 4.11%, the 5-year yield fell 10 bp to 4.17%, the 10-year yield fell 9 bp to 4.41%, and the 30-year yield fell 8 bp to 4.86%. The belly and intermediate sector led, the front end lagged, and the long end participated without becoming the only place the market wanted to own duration. That configuration matters because it points to a rates market that was responding to a mix of softer growth data, lower inflation compensation, and lower real yields, with no one-dimensional flight-to-safety pattern.

The curve also told a more disciplined story than the size of the rally alone. The 2s10s spread flattened 4 bp to 30 bp, while 2s30s flattened 3 bp to 75 bp. A front-end-led rally would normally steepen those spreads; a pure long-end term-premium rally would leave the belly less central. The 5-year and 10-year sectors did the most work. That keeps the market's message close to the Fed reaction-function zone: enough growth and inflation relief to pull the expected path lower, but not enough confidence to force an immediate front-end repricing bigger than the belly.

The quality of the rally improved because nominal yields were not carried only by breakevens. The 10-year TIPS real yield fell 6 bp to 2.23%, while the 10-year breakeven fell 3 bp to 2.18%. Oil was the obvious cross-asset catalyst, with WTI down 4.41% to 69.98 and Brent down 5.14% to 73.12. Lower crude prices reduced the inflation-risk premium that had been sitting in rates after the Middle East shock, but the real-yield decline showed that the move also had a growth and policy-rate component. That distinction is important: an energy-only rally can reverse quickly if crude rebounds; a rally with real-yield participation has a better chance of carrying into the next macro data releases.

The day also had a macro data anchor. New-home sales fell 7.3% in May to a 580K annualized pace, materially below expectations and the weakest level in several months. Housing is the part of the economy most directly exposed to mortgage rates, credit availability and household affordability. When that data weakens on the same day oil falls and real yields drop, the rates market gets a more coherent reason to lower yields. The close was therefore not just an oil trade. It was an oil, housing and real-rate trade, with the 5-year sector acting as the center of gravity.

The main caveat was cross-asset breadth. Equities were mixed, with no decisive risk-off break: the S&P 500 slipped 0.10%, the Nasdaq 100 fell 0.43%, the SOX fell 0.18%, and the VIX fell 4.41%. The dollar rose 0.19% to 101.606 and gold fell 2.51%. Those moves do not describe a panic bid for Treasuries. They describe a market that accepted lower inflation risk and weaker housing while still keeping dollar and policy discipline in place. That is the right frame for the whole close: duration gained credibility, but the market still wants confirmation from inflation data, supply absorption, and dollar behavior.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

The Treasury tape was centered on the belly. The 5-year yield fell 10 bp and the 10-year yield fell 9 bp, compared with a 5 bp decline in the 2-year and an 8 bp decline in the 30-year. That pattern is a market judgment about the middle of the policy path. The 2-year is still anchored by current Fed restrictiveness and the market's reluctance to price near-term relief too aggressively. The 30-year still carries fiscal, term-premium and convexity considerations. The belly can rally when the market sees enough evidence that future nominal growth and inflation pressure are easing without having to price an immediate policy turn.

The curve flattening reinforced that interpretation. At 30 bp, 2s10s was not signaling a new bear-steepening inflation scare or a front-end-only Fed pivot. A 4 bp flattening on a rally says the intermediate sector received more conviction than the policy-sensitive front end. That is consistent with a day when oil relief helped inflation expectations, housing data weakened, and the market still faced a Fed that has not declared victory. The 2-year falling only 5 bp keeps the message grounded: the market took out some path risk, but did not abandon the idea that policy remains restrictive.

Real yields did a meaningful amount of work. The 10-year TIPS yield fell 6 bp to 2.23%, which is a much stronger signal than a breakeven-only rally. Lower real yields speak to expected policy tightness, growth durability and the discount-rate pressure facing equities and credit. When real yields fall alongside nominal yields, the Treasury rally can support duration-sensitive assets without needing equities to collapse. The listed equity close fit that profile: mixed indexes, lower volatility, no broad liquidation. It was a rates-led repricing with enough macro logic to stand on its own.

Fed communication in the policy backdrop leaned against a simple dovish pivot. Treasury Secretary commentary pointed to confidence that inflation is moving lower and named Kevin Warsh as a figure with policy credibility, but the rates market still had to digest a central bank that is not moving on one housing print or one oil move. The front end's smaller decline kept that constraint visible. The market lowered the path at the margin; it did not price an imminent surrender of restrictive policy.

The better way to describe the close is a conditional bull flattening. Conditional, because the 2-year lagged and the dollar strengthened. Bull flattening, because the 5-year and 10-year sectors led yields lower and the curve compressed. The next step is not whether the market can point to one soft data point. It is whether the data sequence can keep real yields falling without reawakening the dollar or supply concession. If PCE and growth revisions validate the oil and housing message, the belly can remain supported. If activity data firm or the dollar keeps rising, the rally will look more like a temporary inflation-risk unwind than a durable shift in policy expectations.

Supply mattered because the strongest Treasury move sat in the same part of the curve that faced an auction test. The 5-year note auction cleared at a 4.200% high yield, with a 4.125% coupon and a price just below par. The result did not derail the rally, which is the most important practical read. A belly-led rates move that survives a same-day 5-year supply event has more credibility than a rally built only in the quiet space before issuance. The auction was not a reason to declare term-premium risk solved; it was a reason to say the market absorbed the belly supply without forcing a reversal.

The calendar data gave the rally a macro base. New-home sales at 580K were a direct rates-sensitive signal. A 7.3% monthly decline after a downwardly revised prior reading argues that higher mortgage rates are having visible bite. Housing does not need to drive every part of the economy to matter for fixed income. It is a transmission channel from policy rates to household demand, builder confidence, bank and nonbank credit, and consumer wealth. Weakness there helps the market believe restrictive policy is working, which lowers the need for the belly of the curve to price a prolonged high-rate regime.

Energy data complicated the oil story but did not overturn it. Crude inventories fell more than expected, yet gasoline and distillate stocks built. The market focused on the price move and the product-build demand signal more than the crude draw alone. That matters for rates because breakevens respond to the inflation impulse, not just the direction of one inventory line. With WTI below 70 and Brent near 73, the market could treat the Middle East risk premium as fading and the product inventory mix as less inflationary. The 3 bp decline in 10-year breakevens was therefore coherent with the energy tape.

Liquidity in the broad market did not show stress. VIX fell despite mixed equity indexes, and listed credit showed only selective risk appetite with no sign of forced selling. That makes the supply picture less threatening. When volatility is falling and investment-grade duration is bid, Treasury auctions can clear without requiring a large risk premium. The 7-year note sale becomes the next practical test because it sits at the boundary between belly and intermediate duration. A clean 7-year result would confirm that the June 24 rally was not just a one-day oil and housing response; a poor result would reintroduce term-premium and concession concerns.

Upcoming GDP, PCE, durable-goods and 7-year supply checks belong in Forward Market Significance and Forward Watch, not as a standalone thesis sector.


The important routing point is that these facts do not need their own sector. The 5-year auction is part of the Treasury curve story because it tested demand exactly where the rally was strongest. New-home sales belong with rates because housing is the cleanest policy-transmission channel in the day's data. Energy inventories belong with inflation compensation because product builds and lower crude prices shaped breakeven pressure. The forward calendar belongs later because PCE, GDP, durable goods and the 7-year auction had not yet been traded through the close.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Credit confirmed lower rates more clearly than risk appetite. LQD rose 0.46%, which fits a day of lower Treasury yields and better duration math. HYG slipped 0.03% and JNK slipped 0.06%, which says high-yield risk did not fully join the rally. EMB rose 0.32%, helped by lower US yields but still facing the firmer dollar. The cross-credit message is therefore tiered: high-quality duration benefited, while lower-rated or more dollar-sensitive risk did not send a broad all-clear.

That tiering is consistent with the broader borrower-access backdrop. Large and high-quality borrowers remain able to use bond markets, with high-grade supply running at a strong June pace and major names still attracting demand. Private credit and more leveraged borrowers face a different landscape. Debt extensions, refinancing delays and asset-specific funding stories show that lower Treasury yields do not automatically repair balance sheets. A rally in the risk-free curve lowers discount rates and coupons at the margin, but it does not remove refinancing cliffs, collateral sensitivity or lender selectivity.

The Fed stress-test result added a constructive bank-capital layer after the close. Large banks remained above minimum capital requirements under a severe recession scenario, with aggregate capital down only 1.6 percentage points even under more than $708bn of hypothetical losses. That is important for credit because banks sit at the center of lending capacity, market-making balance sheet and confidence in financial intermediation. The result should not be treated as a catalyst for the Treasury close because its timing was later than the cash-market move. It is better treated as Forward Market Significance: the funding system entered the next session with a cleaner bank-capital headline than it might have had otherwise.

Bank supervision developments also matter for the credit backdrop. A more streamlined supervisory division and a stress-test result that showed resilience both reduce the immediate fear that bank capital will become a near-term shock. That does not mean credit spreads should tighten mechanically. It means the system did not add a new destabilizing factor on a day when lower yields were already helping duration. For investment-grade credit, that is supportive. For high yield, it is less decisive because default risk, earnings quality and refinancing access still dominate.

The equity market's mixed close reinforces the same read. If credit were fully embracing a risk-on impulse, high-yield ETFs and cyclically sensitive equities would likely have shown stronger confirmation. tech was soft, the S&P was nearly flat, volatility fell, and high-yield ETFs were slightly lower. That leaves credit calm but short of spread euphoria. The market rewarded duration and bank-capital stability while still distinguishing between high-quality borrowers and weaker balance sheets.

The practical implication is that credit should be read through two channels. The first is duration: lower Treasury yields lift investment-grade total returns and make new-issue supply easier to absorb. The second is access: weaker borrowers still need cash-flow, collateral and refinancing proof. June 24 improved the first channel more than the second. The next confirmation will come from whether high-yield spreads and leveraged-credit stories join the rates rally, or whether IG remains the main beneficiary while borrower tiering persists. That distinction remains central for allocation.


One more point is important for the public-private credit bridge: the day did not show a shortage of capital for every borrower. Large high-grade supply, infrastructure private-bond demand and bank stress-test resilience all point to functioning intermediation. The issue is the price and selectivity of that capital. Strong issuers can still finance, but borrowers with refinancing delays, collateral sensitivity or weaker cash-flow visibility remain exposed. That is why the credit read cannot be reduced to LQD up and high yield flat. The rates rally improves the discount-rate backdrop, while the borrower stories keep the market focused on credit quality, maturity walls and lender willingness. In fixed-income terms, the marginal dollar is still moving toward quality duration first and only then toward weaker beta if macro confirmation continues.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Global duration received help from lower oil and lower US yields, but local central-bank constraints remained important. Japan was the clearest example. BOJ communication continued to affirm the possibility of further rate hikes as inflation risks remain above target. Governor Ueda's warning that inflation could exceed the 2% target keeps Japan from fully importing the US duration rally. JGBs can benefit from global yield direction, but the domestic policy impulse still leans toward normalization. That makes Japan a partial participant in US-led duration relief, with domestic policy still setting the constraint.

Korea carried a similar local constraint. Bank of Korea officials highlighted the need for higher rates amid housing and household-debt risks. That is a different channel from oil inflation, but it reaches the same conclusion: lower global energy prices do not automatically free every central bank to ease. If housing and credit excess are domestic policy problems, a global duration rally may actually sharpen the policy dilemma by loosening financial conditions before local imbalances cool.

Singapore sat closer to the benign side of the distribution. Mild inflation supported expectations that policy can remain on hold. In a global fixed-income framework, that gives Singapore a more stable nominal anchor than economies where housing, currency or wage risks force central banks to sound hawkish. The region is differentiated: Singapore's inflation profile supports patience, Japan and Korea remain constrained, India signals no near-term hike, and Thailand is expected to hold to support growth even as some neighbors stay more restrictive.

Europe also did not give a uniform dovish signal. ECB speakers kept price stability at the center of the policy debate even as oil prices fell. Lower oil helps, but it does not erase the inflation target or the pass-through risk that central banks must monitor. European fiscal politics and budget negotiations left ratings and markets calm in the broader policy backdrop while keeping term-premium sensitivity alive. That keeps Bunds and peripheral spreads sensitive to more than the US Treasury close.

The global lesson from June 24 is that duration can rally together without all central banks sharing the same reaction function. Lower oil is a common support. Lower US real yields are a common discount-rate support. But local inflation, housing, debt and currency constraints decide how much of that support transfers into each curve. That is why the dollar's firmness matters. A firmer DXY at 101.606 tells non-US central banks that imported financial conditions are not unambiguously easier. It also keeps pressure on countries where currency weakness can feed inflation or funding stress.

For the next few sessions, global duration needs two confirmations. First, oil must stay low enough to keep inflation risk premium compressed. Second, US data must not force the front end back toward a higher-for-longer path. If both happen, global curves can keep receiving support even where local central banks remain cautious. If the dollar strengthens further or Japan/Korea policy rhetoric intensifies, the US rally will look more local and less exportable.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging markets received a helpful US rates impulse but not a complete liquidity easing. EMB rose 0.32%, which is consistent with lower Treasury yields and lower oil volatility helping hard-currency debt. The problem is that DXY rose 0.19%. A firmer dollar limits the breadth of EM relief because local central banks, external borrowers and commodity-linked assets all still have to manage currency sensitivity. Lower US yields are powerful, but they are not the only variable in EM fixed income.

China and the export channel were central to the EM narrative. The market context flagged a view that China's export glut can support EM bonds by exporting disinflation to recipient economies. That is plausible for countries importing cheaper goods, because lower tradables inflation gives central banks more room to hold or ease. It is less straightforward for countries competing with Chinese exports or facing pressure on domestic industry. The fixed-income implication is selective disinflation, not blanket EM bullishness.

Thailand illustrated the growth side of that selectivity. Expectations for the central bank to hold rates reflected the need to support growth even as neighbors face different inflation and currency choices. India moved in a different direction, with the RBI governor giving a strong signal against rate hikes. Mexico's early June inflation eased more than expected, while Chile held rates steady and signaled caution as inflation risks ease. Brazil remained more complex, with investors looking for bargains after bond losses while inflation risks still influence the policy path. Argentina was able to raise dollars below 7%, highlighting that market access can improve country by country even when the broader dollar is not soft.

Commodity transmission also stayed mixed. Oil's sharp fall helped energy-importing EM and reduced global inflation anxiety. Copper, however, remained pressured by dollar strength and hawkish Fed concerns. That split matters because EM is not a single commodity exposure. Lower oil is positive for importers and disinflation trades. Lower copper can point to weaker industrial demand and China pressure. A stronger dollar can offset both by tightening financial conditions for external borrowers.

Private-credit and corporate-bond stories reinforced the country-by-country and issuer-by-issuer theme. India's Shapoorji Pallonji seeking a fresh bond extension shows that lower global yields do not erase refinancing challenges. Large infrastructure and LNG private-bond transactions show that long-term capital is still available for assets with scale and contracted cash-flow appeal. The market is open, but it is not open equally. That is an important EM and cross-border credit message: rates relief helps, but credit selection still dominates.

The EM conclusion is therefore constructive but not euphoric. EMB's gain confirms that lower US yields had a positive transmission channel. The dollar's rise, copper weakness and local policy divergence prevent a broader all-clear. For EM duration to extend, the market needs lower US real yields to persist and DXY to stop tightening financial conditions. For hard-currency credit, the key is whether the rates rally improves issuance and refinancing access beyond the strongest sovereign and corporate names. June 24 opened that door, but did not prove the whole borrower base can walk through it.

## Cross asset

Cross-asset confirmation was strongest in energy, inflation compensation and volatility. WTI fell to 69.98 and Brent to 73.12, taking the market back toward levels that reduced the immediate geopolitical inflation premium. The 10-year breakeven fell to 2.18%, validating the inflation-risk channel. VIX fell 4.41%, suggesting the Treasury rally did not depend on a disorderly equity selloff. Together, those signals make the rates move cleaner than a simple safe-haven bid.

Equities were less enthusiastic. The S&P 500 slipped 0.10%, the Nasdaq 100 fell 0.43%, and the SOX fell 0.18%. Those are modest declines, but they matter because lower real yields usually support long-duration equities if growth fear is not rising. The muted equity response says investors were not ready to convert lower yields into a broad risk rally. AI and technology remain important because market positioning is crowded and capital spending expectations are high. Micron's strong forecast and broader AI infrastructure demand can support parts of the equity market, but high valuation and financing sensitivity mean lower yields are helpful without being decisive.

Gold's 2.51% decline also fit the dollar and real-rate nuance. Even though real yields fell, the dollar strengthened and geopolitical fear eased as oil fell and Hormuz traffic concerns moderated. Gold did not act like the market was buying inflation hedges or safety at any price. That supports the thesis that June 24 was a disinflation and duration rally, not a broad debasement or crisis trade.

Listed credit added another layer. LQD's gain was the cleanest cross-asset confirmation because it benefits directly from lower Treasury yields and high-grade duration. HYG and JNK were flat to slightly lower, which keeps credit-risk appetite from confirming the full move. EMB rose, which confirms some relief for hard-currency EM, but the dollar's gain keeps the read conditional. Cross-asset signals therefore lined up around duration quality, not speculative risk.

Oil remains the central swing variable because it touches inflation, breakevens, central-bank rhetoric and EM terms of trade. The sharp fall in crude helped explain why breakevens declined and why nominal yields could rally even with the dollar firm. But oil's message must be watched alongside product inventories and geopolitical logistics. A crude draw with product builds can still be disinflationary if the price move and demand signal dominate. A rebound in crude would test whether the rates market over-relied on one cross-asset input.

The best summary is that cross-assets confirmed the direction of the Treasury move while limiting the size of the conclusion. Energy, breakevens, real yields, VIX and LQD all joined the rally. Equities were mixed, high yield lagged, DXY rose and gold fell. That is a credible fixed-income rally, but not a broad macro surrender. It says the market bought duration because inflation and housing evidence improved, while still respecting policy uncertainty, supply risk and dollar discipline.

## Forward Market Significance

The next market tests are specific. PCE price revisions, final GDP, durable goods orders, Kansas Fed surveys and the 7-year auction will determine whether the June 24 rally can extend. The market needs confirmation across two dimensions: inflation pressure and real activity. Lower oil already helped inflation compensation, but official inflation revisions can either validate or challenge that relief. Weak housing already showed rates-sensitive strain, but durable goods and regional manufacturing can say whether that strain is broadening.

The 7-year auction is the cleanest supply test. The 5-year sale allowed a belly-led rally, and the 7-year sector sits close enough to the intermediate part of the curve to test demand. A clean auction would reinforce the idea that investors are willing to own duration after the oil and housing move. A weak auction would revive the term-premium and concession story, especially if activity data are firm. Supply can decide whether rallies hold their range even when it does not dominate the market every day.

The dollar is the main cross-border check. A firmer DXY limited EM breadth on June 24 even as EMB rose. If DXY keeps rising, global duration and EM credit will struggle to turn a US rates rally into broader easing of financial conditions. If the dollar fades while oil stays lower, EM hard-currency debt and select local rates can extend relief. That makes the dollar an equal partner with oil in the forward setup.

Bank stress-test results should be watched through credit transmission, with Treasury causality kept secondary. The official result supports confidence that large banks can keep lending under severe stress assumptions. The market still needs to see whether that translates into bank equity performance, credit spreads, capital-return expectations and funding costs. If bank credit improves while IG duration stays bid, the rates rally gains a financial-conditions support. If high yield and borrower-specific refinancing stories stay weak, credit tiering remains the dominant message.

Oil must also hold the line. The rates market benefited from a sharp crude decline and lower breakevens. If crude stabilizes around lower levels, central-bank inflation language can soften at the margin and breakeven compression can persist. If crude rebounds on geopolitical headlines or supply risks, the June 24 move will be vulnerable because part of the nominal rally was explicitly tied to inflation-risk relief. Product inventory builds give the market some comfort, but price persistence is what matters for inflation expectations.

The final implication is balanced. June 24 improved the fixed-income setup materially: real yields fell, breakevens fell, the belly led lower, housing weakened, oil dropped, IG duration rallied and bank-capital news was constructive. The market still needs confirmation from inflation data, supply, dollar behavior and credit breadth. Until those arrive, the correct stance is not to dismiss the rally, and not to overstate it. It is a credible duration rally with clear confirmation hurdles.

section_summaries.summary: June 24 delivered a credible belly-led Treasury rally, supported by lower oil, weak housing, softer breakevens and lower real yields. The move was cleaner than the prior close, yet mixed equities, dollar firmness and selective credit kept it conditional. The next proof points are 7Y supply, inflation revisions, durable goods and dollar behavior.
section_summaries.market_read: Duration gained support without a broad equity panic.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The move had better quality than an energy-only unwind, but dollar firmness kept it conditional.
section_summaries.next_check: PCE, GDP revisions and dollar behavior.

section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: The rates section shows the 5Y and 10Y sectors doing most of the work as real yields and inflation compensation declined together. Weak new-home sales and a clean 5Y auction helped the move, while energy inventory details shaped breakevens. The 7Y auction and PCE/GDP/durable-goods checks decide whether the belly bid extends.
section_summaries.market_read: Belly-led rally survived 5Y supply and gained support from housing weakness.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: It keeps auction and released macro evidence inside the rates transmission channel.
section_summaries.next_check: 7Y auction, PCE, GDP and durable-goods confirmation.

section_summaries.summary: Credit improved mainly where duration and quality mattered. Investment-grade exposure benefited from lower yields and bank stress-test resilience, while high-yield ETFs lagged and borrower-level details kept refinancing, lender selectivity and maturity walls in focus. The key follow-up is whether spreads and primary demand confirm the rates-led improvement.
section_summaries.market_read: Credit confirmed high-quality duration more than speculative risk appetite.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Borrower tiering remains the key credit theme.
section_summaries.next_check: Bank-credit performance and HY spread response.

section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Global duration received help from lower oil and US yields, but local policy constraints stayed visible. Japan, Korea and the ECB still faced inflation, housing, currency or credibility limits, while Singapore looked more stable. The section frames the U.S. rally as exportable only where domestic inflation and FX risk allow it.
section_summaries.market_read: The US rally is exportable only where local inflation and currency risks allow.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Global curves remain differentiated even when oil falls.
section_summaries.next_check: BOJ, Korea, ECB rhetoric and dollar direction.

section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: EM hard-currency debt benefited from lower US yields, but dollar firmness, copper weakness, commodity dispersion and country-specific policy divergence kept relief selective. The section separates oil-importer support from dollar-liquidity pressure and asks whether EMB, DXY and local FX can turn a rates tailwind into broader sovereign risk appetite.
section_summaries.market_read: EM relief was selective, not a single beta trade.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Lower oil helps importers, but DXY still governs external financing conditions.

section_summaries.summary: Cross-assets supported the Treasury rally through lower oil, softer breakevens, lower real yields, a lower VIX and investment-grade duration demand. Mixed equities, high-yield lag, gold weakness and a firmer dollar kept the message narrower than a full risk rally. The next check is whether risk breadth improves without oil reversing.
section_summaries.market_read: Cross-assets validated the direction of lower yields without confirming broad risk euphoria.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The rally is most credible in quality duration.
section_summaries.next_check: Oil stability, equity breadth and HY confirmation.

section_summaries.summary: The forward section turns the close into concrete checks: inflation and growth revisions, durable goods, Kansas Fed surveys, 7Y supply, oil persistence, dollar direction and credit response to bank-capital news. Those items decide whether the better duration setup becomes a broader FI relief trade or remains a belly-led repricing.
section_summaries.market_read: The close created a better setup but still needs confirmation.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Supply, inflation and dollar checks can either extend or cap the rally.
section_summaries.next_check: PCE, GDP, durable goods, 7Y supply, oil and DXY.
